fr:title.text: How to get a logic from a natural deduction system

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: How to get a logic from a natural deduction system

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Last time, we met three natural deduction systems: NJ, NM, and NK. We spent the most time on NJ, and that will continue to be the case. We'll think of NM and NK basically as variations on NJ. Just remember that they differ from NJ in two different ways: NM by lacking the rule of \bot E, and NK by having in addition the rule of LEM.

This week, we're going to start thinking about the logics determined by these proof systems. Remember, a logic is a set of arguments. So we need a way, given a proof system, to determine a particular set of arguments. Here as everywhere in logic, there are options; but we're going to use a standard way to get the job done.

Recall that the idea behind proof systems like natural deduction is this: we say what it takes for a proof to be a proof of a particular argument, and then we count an argument as valid as long as there is at least one proof that is a proof of it. It's the reverse of what we did with valuations, where we said what it takes for a valuation to be a counterexample to an argument, and then count an argument as invalid when there is at least one valuation that is a counterexample to it.
So what we need is just a story about what it takes for a natural deduction proof to be a proof of an argument. That's where this lecture starts.

From any natural deduction proof, we can determine a single argument \Gamma \;/\; A as follows: let A be the conclusion of the proof, and let \Gamma be the set of formulas that occur in the proof as open assumptions. Remember: sets don't care about multiplicity! So it doesn't matter if something occurs as an open assumption once or twenty times; the only thing that matters is whether it occurs at least once, or not at all. Any proof at all determines an argument in this way; we'll call it the smallest argument of the proof. That is:

Given a proof in a natural deduction system, the smallest argument of that proof is the argument \Gamma \;/\; A determined as follows:
\Gamma is the set of sentences that are open assumptions in the proof, and
A is the proof's conclusion.
Using that, we can say what it takes for a proof to be a proof of an argument:

A proof is a proof of an argument \Sigma \;/\; B iff, where the proof's smallest argument is \Gamma \;/\; A:
\Gamma \subseteq \Sigma , and
B is A.

To put that same idea in different words: a proof counts as a proof of \Sigma \;/\; B when the proof's conclusion is B and the proof's open assumptions are all among the members of \Sigma . It's totally fine if \Sigma has other members as well, but it has to contain all the proof's open assumptions.,

Because of this, every proof is a proof of very many different arguments. Again, this is just like our counterexamples were: a single valuation can be a counterexample to many different arguments at once, and now a single proof can be a proof of many different arguments at once.

The idea behind this way of thinking is this. Think of an argument \Sigma / B as saying that, if we established everything in \Sigma , that would be enough to work with to allow us to establish B. Now, if we have a proof of B whose open assumptions are all in \Sigma , then that shows us a way to do this. If we established everything in \Sigma , then we'd have established all the open assumptions of the proof, since all those assumptions are in \Sigma . And the proof shows us how, if we took those assumptions for granted, we could reason to the conclusion B. So if we've actually established those assumptions, rather than just taking them for granted, the proof would finish the job for us, showing us how to establish B. From this perspective, it's totally fine if there are extra things in \Sigma that aren't open assumptions in the proof; that doesn't get in the way at all.

You can see the smallest argument \Gamma \;/\; A of a proof as being the common core of all the arguments that the proof is a proof of: every argument that the proof is a proof of has a set of premises that includes everything in \Gamma , and has the conclusion A. It's absolutely crucial to remember, though, that any proof is a proof of many arguments, not just its smallest argument.
Here's an example proof from last time:
What arguments is this a proof of?

Well, the conclusion of this proof is p \rightarrow (p \land p), so every argument that it's a proof of has that conclusion as well. And the open assumptions of this proof are p \lor q, p, and q \rightarrow p, so every argument that it's a proof of has these among its premises. And that's all we need. So this is a proof of very many (in fact, infinitely many) different arguments. For example, it is a proof of p \lor q, p, q \rightarrow p \;/\; p \rightarrow (p \land p); and it is also a proof of r, t \rightarrow s, \neg p, p \lor q, p, q \rightarrow p \;/\; p \rightarrow (p \land p). Indeed, for any set \Gamma of sentences, this is a proof of \Gamma , p \lor q, p, q \rightarrow p \;/\; p \rightarrow (p \land p).

It is not, however, a proof of p \lor q, q \rightarrow p \;/\; p \rightarrow (p \land p). The reason is that the proof has an open assumption, p, that is not among the premises of this argument. Similarly, this is not a proof of r, p \lor q \;/\; p \rightarrow (p \land p), for basically the same reason.
Here's another example proof from last time:

This has the conclusion (p \rightarrow ((p \land r) \rightarrow q) \rightarrow (p \rightarrow q)), and the sole open assumption r. So it is a proof of any argument \Gamma \;/\; (p \rightarrow ((p \land r) \rightarrow q) \rightarrow (p \rightarrow q)), as long as \Gamma includes r. For example, it is a proof of r \;/\; (p \rightarrow ((p \land r) \rightarrow q) \rightarrow (p \rightarrow q)), and it is a proof of p \rightarrow p, r \;/\; (p \rightarrow ((p \land r) \rightarrow q) \rightarrow (p \rightarrow q)).

However, it is not a proof of q \;/\; (p \rightarrow ((p \land r) \rightarrow q) \rightarrow (p \rightarrow q)) or of \;/\; (p \rightarrow ((p \land r) \rightarrow q) \rightarrow (p \rightarrow q)), since the open assumption r doesn't occur among the premises in either of these arguments.
One final example proof from last week:

This has the conclusion (p \land q) \rightarrow p, and it has no open assumptions. So it is a proof of absolutely any argument of the form \Gamma \;/\; (p \land q) \rightarrow p; since there are no assumptions at all, we say they're all among \Gamma no matter what \Gamma is. In particular, this holds even if \Gamma is empty; so this proof is a proof of \;/\; (p \land q) \rightarrow p. It's also a proof of, for example, (p \land q) \rightarrow p \;/\; (p \land q) \rightarrow p, and of every other argument with the same conclusion.
This last example reveals something: if you want to have a proof of an argument with no premises, you need to have a proof with no open assumptions.

Now we have three natural deduction proof systems, and we know how to tell when a proof is a proof of an argument. This gives us three logics (yes, they're all different), via the following definition:

fr:title.text: Logic determined by a proof system

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Logic determined by a proof system

fr:mainmatter.html:p: The logic determined by a particular proof system is the logic that contains an argument iff that argument has a proof in the system.
Note that there's no guarantee in general that two different proof systems must always result in two different logics. For example, imagine we added this rule to NK:

The resulting proof system would be different from NK, since there is no rule like this in NK; we would get a bunch of new proofs using this rule that aren't proofs in NK. However, it turns out that the resulting proof system still determines classical logic; this particular addition to the proof system hasn't changed the resulting logic. That is, every argument that has a proof in this larger system also has a proof in NK. There are more proofs in the larger system, but not proofs of anything new.

A bit of jargon we won't use this semester, but can lead you down a rabbit hole if you want: when a rule and a proof system are like this---adding the rule to the proof system doesn't change the resulting logic---the rule is called admissible in the proof system. To say that the logics determined by NM, NJ, and NK are all different, then, is to say that \bot E is not admissible in NM, and LEM is not admissible in NJ.

The logic determined by NJ in this way is called intuitionistic logic. The logic determined by NM in this way is called minimal logic. And the logic determined by NK in this way is good old classical logic.

That claim about classical logic is a substantial one: I really am saying that the logic determined by the proof system NK is exactly the same one we defined via matrices weeks ago! You should not think that that's obvious; it very much is not.
The other two, though, we'll treat as definitional, as saying which logics we mean by the names.
Intuitionistic logic is the logic determined by the proof system NJ. Minimal logic is the logic determined by the proof system NM.
Let's take a look at some of the similarities and differences between these logics, and see how they fit together.

The first thing to notice is that we have a simple order of strength among these three. Every NM proof is an NJ proof, and every NJ proof is an NK proof. So if an argument is valid in minimal logic, it has an NM proof, which means it has an NJ proof, and so is valid in intuitionistic logic too; that is to say, minimal logic is weaker than intuitionistic logic. For the same kind of reason, intuitionistic logic is weaker than classical logic.

In fact, they are properly weaker, in both cases. For example, p, 
eg p \;/\; q is valid in intuitionistic but not minimal logic, and 
eg 
eg p \;/\; p is valid in classical but not intuitionistic logic.
How do minimal and intuitionistic logics compare to our other logics? Here's the situation:

You might note that classical logic seems to play a special role here: every logic that we've explored is weaker than it! That's not entirely a coincidence. Classical logic has a property called Post completeness: this means that the only logic that is reflexive, monotonic, transitive, substitution invariant, and properly stronger than classical logic is the logic that includes every argument. As many widely-studied logics are indeed reflexive, monotonic, transitive, and substitution invariant, this means that they won't be properly stronger than classical logic. This doesn't mean, though, that they'll be weaker! But we won't look this semester at any example logics that are not weaker than classical logic.

Outside this side note, that is. One of the coolest logics out there that's not weaker than classical logic is called Abelian logic. This is a logic that's been studied a fair bit at Monash in particular; see for example this paper, written by two (at the time) Monash-associated logicians. The characteristic way Abelian logic ends up not weaker than classical logic: it has ((p \rightarrow q) \rightarrow q) \rightarrow p as a theorem. This is not a theorem of classical logic (although ((p \rightarrow \bot ) \rightarrow \bot ) \rightarrow p is).

Recall four properties that a logic might or might not have: reflexivity, monotonicity, transitivity, and substitution invariance. We've seen that every logic given by a matrix has all four of these properties. What about logics given by natural deduction systems?

First, think about reflexivity. As long as we allow ourselves to assume any sentence at all, then we will always have a proof of A \;/\; A, no matter what sentence A is: just the assumption of A constitutes such a proof. So reflexivity is assured. If we were to work with a system with restrictions on what could be assumed, then there would be a question about reflexivity. But we haven't looked at any such systems.

Second, think about monotonicity. Suppose we have a proof of \Gamma \;/\; A. Then that proof itself is also a proof of \Sigma \;/\; A for any \Sigma where \Gamma is a subset of \Sigma . This is because of how we've defined what it is to be a proof of an argument. So any logic determined by a proof system in the way we're doing it here will be monotonic.

Third, think about transitivity. Suppose we have a proof of \Gamma \;/\; A and a proof of \Gamma , A \;/\; B. The question is: given that much, must we always have a proof of \Gamma \;/\; B? Yes, we must; here's why. Consider the proof of \Gamma , A \;/\; B---call this proof P---and think about its open assumptions. We don't know what these are, but we do know that each of them must either be in \Gamma or be A. If they're all in \Gamma , we're all set: the proof P is already also a proof of \Gamma \;/\; A, which is what we're after.

But they might not all be in \Gamma . In particular, there can also be open assumptions of A in the proof P, and A might not be in \Gamma . If this is the case, then P is not yet a proof of \Gamma \;/\; A. But we can make use of the other proof we've assumed we have: the one of \Gamma \;/\; A.

Imagine plugging a copy of this other proof of \Gamma \;/\; A in at the top of P, wherever there is an open assumption of A. So instead of assuming A in P, we'll instead prove it via the proof of \Gamma \;/\; A we have. If we do this for every open assumption of A in P, we'll end up with a new larger proof of B, and now one where all its open assumptions are in \Gamma . That is, we'll have a proof of \Gamma \;/\; B. So the logics determined by our natural deduction proof systems are transitive.

That was notably more complex than reflexivity or monotonicity, so let's look at an example to see how it works. It's important to recall, though, that just one example isn't enough to show that transitivity holds. This needs to work for every possible example. That's why the schematic and general reasoning above was important. This one example is just to give the flavour; but you should look over it while keeping an eye on the general reasoning, to convince yourself this kind of thing is indeed always possible.
This example uses only rules from NM, so it works in any of our three systems. Here are two simple proofs in NM:

The first of these is a proof of p \rightarrow q, p, r \;/\; q, and the second of p \rightarrow q, p, r, q \;/\; r \land q. So if the logic this system determines is transitive, there had better be a proof somewhere in the system of p \rightarrow q, p, r \;/\; r \land q. And indeed there is: we take the first example proof here, and plug it into the top of the second, replacing each open assumption of q in the second proof with a copy of the first proof. The result looks like this:

See how, in this result, the bottom is made up of the second example proof above, while the top bits are filled in with the first proof. This result is a proof of p \rightarrow q, p, r \;/\; r \land q; just what we needed.

The key to making sure this is possible is this: all of our rules for building larger proofs out of smaller proofs (which is to say, all of our rules other than assumption, \top I, and LEM) are always applicable based only on the conclusions of the smaller proofs they build on. In the above example, the rule \land I doesn't care whether its inputs are assumptions, as they are in the original example, or proofs that do actual work, as they are in the finished product. The rule \land I acts on them just the same either way. So when we plug in full proofs for open assumptions, we can leave everything else alone and be confident that it will all work out: the result will still be a proof.

It's worth pausing here to think through how this would look with rules that do some discharging hanging around in the proofs. I promise it will work! But it is worth thinking through an example or two.

Finally, substitution invariance. This one is more straightforward: applying a substitution to an entire proof always results in another proof. So whenever we have a proof of \Gamma \;/\; A, if we want a proof of some substitution instance of that argument, we can just apply the appropriate substitution to the proof itself, and we're all set. To be sure this works, you need to check that it works for every rule. After all, imagine we had a rule like this:

Think about adding this rule to NJ. Then we reach a proof system that does include a proof of p \lor q \;/\; p. But this proof system still does not include any proof of r \lor s \;/\; r. The reason is that the rule FAKE RULE is not itself substitution invariant: it applies only to the particular sentences that occur in it. The actual rules of NM, NJ, and NK, though, are not like that. Every actual rule in these systems applies to every substitution instance of anything it applies to. So the logics these systems determine are indeed substitution invariant.

In philosophy, the words "realism" and "antirealism" get used for lots of different distinctions. But one pretty well-established use is connected to the difference between classical and intuitionistic logic, and it's that use I want to tell you a little about here.
One way to get into the idea is to think about the following question: how many seconds old was Socrates when he drank the hemlock?

Context: Socrates was an ancient Athenian philosopher who was sentenced to death by drinking hemlock in 399 BCE. He is mainly remembered through the writings of his student Plato. Socrates didn't write anything himself, and indeed is portrayed in Plato's dialogue Phaedrus as not being super-friendly to writing, saying (tr. Robin Waterfield):
"[T]here’s something odd about writing, Phaedrus, which makes it exactly like painting. The offspring of painting stand there as if alive, but if you ask them a question they maintain an aloof silence. It’s the same with written words: you might think they were speaking as if they had some intelligence, but if you want an explanation of any of the things they’re saying and you ask them about it, they just go on and on for ever giving the same single piece of information. Once any account has been written down, you find it all over the place, hobnobbing with completely inappropriate people no less than with those who understand it, and completely failing to know who it should and shouldn’t talk to."

It seems like a pretty safe bet that nobody will ever know the answer to the question of how many seconds old Socrates was when he drank the hemlock. Even so, some people think the question still has a true answer. The fact that nobody will ever know the answer is beside the point; there is still some number that is the true answer. This is one thing some people mean by "realism": the belief that there are unknowable truths. On the other hand, others think that there can't really be any truth of the matter here, without some method of discovering it. Maybe we could know that Socrates was more than a billion seconds (about 32 years) old, and less than three billion seconds old, so those things can be true. But if the exact number of seconds is really unknowable, then people in this second camp would maintain that there is no truth of the matter at all. This second view is sometimes called "antirealism".

One aspect of the debate between realists and antirealists focuses on the so-called law of excluded middle, or LEM. LEM is the schema A \lor 
eg A. As we saw last time, the difference between NJ and NK is precisely that NK contains a rule allowing us to prove any instance of LEM from nothing at all. That shows us that, in classical logic, any argument \;/\; A \lor 
eg A is valid, since every such argument has a proof. (You could also recall our presentation of classical logic via Boolean valuations, and check that whatever sentence A is, in every Boolean valuation v we have v(A \lor 
eg A) = \mathsf {\mathbf {T}}.) That is to say, every instance of LEM is a classical theorem.

In intuitionistic logic, by contrast, not every instance of LEM is a theorem. For example, the argument \;/\; p \lor 
eg p is not valid in intuitionistic logic. Try all you like, you will never find a proof in NJ of p \lor 
eg p without some open assumptions. It would be wrong to conclude from this that no instance of LEM is an intuitionistic theorem, however; some are. For example, \;/\; (p ightarrow p) \lor 
eg (p ightarrow p) is intuitionistically valid. So it's not that intuitionistic logic disagrees with classical logic about every instance of LEM. It's rather just that classical logic takes all such instances to be theorems, and intuitionistic logic takes only some to be.

This is one reason that the difference between these logics gets connected to the difference between realism and antirealism. According to realism, either Socrates was exactly 2.25 billion seconds old when he drank the hemlock, or he wasn't exactly 2.25 billion seconds old when he drank the hemlock. That we can't establish which is true doesn't matter; still it's either yes or no. On the other hand, antirealists want to resist this claim. They wouldn't want to say that either he was exactly 2.25 billion seconds old or he wasn't. Since we don't have any way to tell, many antirealists would not want to go along with the disjunction. As one antirealist puts the point: "Intuitionistic logic is most celebrated for rejecting the law of excluded middle...It does so on the ground that we may not be in a position to judge correct either an assertion or a denial of A". (Dummett, "'Yes', 'no', and 'can't say'") Note that by "rejecting the law of excluded middle", Dummett must mean rejecting some instances of excluded middle, not rejecting all instances of it. As we've seen, some instances of it are intuitionistic theorems.

The proofs of NJ are used by antirealists in this mould as a formal surrogate for the kinds of deductive reasoning people actually do to try to gain knowledge. On this approach, then, the fact that there is no proof in NJ of the argument \;/\; p \lor \neg p fits well with the antirealist refusal to accept claims like "Either Socrates was exactly 2.25 billion seconds old when he drank the hemlock or he wasn't". We have no way to demonstrate this disjunction, antirealists would maintain, and so no right to assert it.

fr:title.text: Proof and meaning, truth value and meaning

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Proof and meaning, truth value and meaning

fr:mainmatter.html:p: I've described both valuations and proofs as ways of giving meaning to our languages. But what does that mean? There's a ton of philosophical work about this, and I won't be able to do more here than scratch the surface; but I'll try to give a broad-brush overview.

On one way of thinking about meaning, the meaning of a piece of language is mainly a matter of what part or parts of the world that language picks out. You can think of this way of handling meaning as a kind of generalization from cases like proper names. "Carl" is the name of my (most excellent) dog, and it might seem like that kind of thing is all there is to the meaning of the name "Carl": if you know who it picks out, you know all there is to know about the meaning of the name.

Where this kind of theory of meaning tends to get a bit stickier is with words like "very", or "because", or "and"; what things in the world do these words pick out? That's not a rhetorical question: in fact, linguists, together with philosophers and logicians, have developed sophisticated and interesting answers to this question. Many of those answers go beyond what we can see this semester, although the semester will leave you well-prepared to pursue such answers in the future, if you want.

But we can see, at least for a word like "and", something of what an answer might be like by thinking about our structures from earlier in the semester. Perhaps a truth table, like the ones we've seen for \land in our various structures, can be the right kind of thing for a word like "and" to pick out. Pursuing this line of thought leads to the development of what's often called "truth-conditional semantics", and this is currently the mainstream method for giving theories of meaning in linguistics and philosophy.

Truth-conditional semantics, though, is not the only option for thinking about linguistic meaning. On another way of thinking, the meaning of a piece of language is mainly a matter of how that language is used. You can think of this way of handling meaning as a kind of generalization from cases like "hello". You surely know the meaning of "hello". If you take a minute to try to explain what that meaning is, you'll probably find yourself describing how the word is used: it's something you say to greet someone. It can seem that, if you know exactly how "hello" is used, you know exactly what it means.

This kind of theory works well, too, for words like "and", but now it tends to invoke something like our introduction and elimination rules, rather than structures. When can you assert "A and B"? Whenever you can assert both A and B. That idea can be seen in our NJ rules for conjunction. Again, there is difficulty with words like "very" and "because"; but again, there are approaches that people take. This way of proceeding leads to an approach known as "proof-theoretic semantics".

Sticking points for proof-theoretic semantics include words like proper names. Again, think about the name "Carl" for my dog. When do we use that name? It's not a matter of proximity to Carl. For example, I'm in the office as I'm writing this, and Carl is not with me at the moment, but here I am, using the name Carl perfectly sensibly; and you understand me, even though Carl is probably not with you at the moment either. (If he is, give him some peanut butter! He loves it.) The easiest way to describe the use of "Carl" is to say that it's a name we use to refer to Carl; that is, to give the same answer that the truth-theoretic semanticist gives.

As I've indicated here, truth-conditional theories of meaning tend to sit well with model-theoretic approaches to language; and proof-conditional theories of meaning tend to sit well with proof-theoretic approaches to language. This is one reason it can be useful to see both kinds of approach to logic, as we're doing in this subject. Each of these ways of thinking about meaning has places where it works more smoothly and places where it works less smoothly. It's not completely obvious how to combine the two---and they are not the only options anyhow---but perhaps combining them is a useful way forward. For now, though, I'll leave it there.

Intuitionistic logic and minimal logic are weaker than classical logic, but in a different kind of way from the other logics we've considered. A lot of the interest in intuitionistic logic is driven by features of its implication, so I'll start there. Unlike all of K3, LP, FDE, but like classical logic, both intuitionistic and minimal logics obey principles called modus ponens and conditional proof. These are two properties often taken to be key to the meaning of implication, so it's worth pausing here to explore them a bit.
An argument is an instance of modus ponens when it has the form A \rightarrow B, A \;/\; B.

So, for example, the argument (p \land 
eg r) ightarrow (s \lor r), p \land 
eg r \;/\; s \lor r is an instance of modus ponens. One thing many thinkers take as a constraint on a good theory of implication is that every instance of modus ponens should come out to be valid. Suppose that the premises (p \land 
eg r) ightarrow (s \lor r) and p \land 
eg r hold. Since we've supposed that p \land 
eg r holds, it seems that everything it implies should also hold. And we've supposed that it implies s \lor r. Thus, it seems to many that s \lor r must follow from these premises.
Classical logic validates modus ponens---by which I mean that it validates every instance of modus ponens. Recall that implication works in the Boolean structure according to this table:

Since we arrive at classical logic by using the matrix that designates just \mathsf {\mathbf {T}} in this structure, to have a classical counterexample to p \rightarrow q, p \;/\; q, we would need to assign both p \rightarrow q and p the value \mathsf {\mathbf {T}} while assigning q the value \mathsf {\mathbf {F}}; as the above table shows, this is not possible. So p \rightarrow q, p \;/\; q is valid in classical logic, and since classical logic is substitution invariant, this means that all instances of modus ponens are classically valid as well.
Now, in the strong Kleene structure, implication works according to this table:

If we designate just \mathsf {\mathbf {T}} to reach the logic K3, you can see that the reasoning works just as in classical logic: there is no strong Kleene valuation v where v(p \rightarrow q) = v(p) = \mathsf {\mathbf {T}}, but where v(q) \neq \mathsf {\mathbf {T}}. So p \rightarrow q, p \;/\; q is valid in K3 as well. However, things are different when it comes to LP. Recall that LP can be specified by a matrix that uses the strong Kleene structure, but with both \mathsf {\mathbf {T}} and \mathsf {\mathbf {*}} counting as designated values. Then there is a valuation w with w(p) = \mathsf {\mathbf {*}} and w(q) = \mathsf {\mathbf {F}}; that gives us w(p \rightarrow q) = \mathsf {\mathbf {*}} by the above table. So w is a counterexample to p \rightarrow q, p \;/\; q, and thus modus ponens is not valid in LP. As FDE is weaker than LP, this means that modus ponens must not be valid in FDE either.

When it comes to NJ, the reasoning around modus ponens is different. For any A and B, there is a proof of A \rightarrow B, A \;/\; B, consisting of just assumptions of A \rightarrow B and A, followed by a single step of \rightarrow E. For example, the following proof is a proof of p \rightarrow q, p \;/\; q:

As this same idea would work for any other sentences, every instance of modus ponens is valid in minimal logic, and thus in intuitionistic and classical logics as well. So minimal and intuitionistic logics are like classical logic and K3, and unlike LP and FDE in this regard.
Conditional proof is not an argument like modus ponens; it is a rule.
A rule instance is an instance of conditional proof when it fits the following form:

That is, when a logic obeys conditional proof, whenever we have a valid argument in the logic with at least one premise, we can reach a new valid argument in the logic by removing one of its premises, and replacing the conclusion with a particular implication: the one that has the removed premise as antecedent and the original conclusion as consequent. (Recall that "antecedent" and "consequent" are special names for the left and right components of an implication, respectively.) Just as with modus ponens, some logics do obey conditional proof and others do not.

For example, classical logic does obey conditional proof. Imagine that we have a classical counterexample to \Gamma \;/\; A \rightarrow B. This must be a Boolean valuation v that takes every sentence in \Gamma to \mathsf {\mathbf {T}}, and takes A \rightarrow B to \mathsf {\mathbf {F}}. The only way, though, that v(A \rightarrow B) = \mathsf {\mathbf {F}} is if v(A) = \mathsf {\mathbf {T}} and v(B) = \mathsf {\mathbf {F}}. So v is also a classical counterexample to \Gamma , A \;/\; B. That shows that whenever an argument of the form \Gamma \;/\; A \rightarrow B is invalid in classical logic, the corresponding argument \Gamma , A \;/\; B is also invalid in classical logic, which shows that classical logic obeys the rule. By identical reasoning, LP also obeys conditional proof.

K3 and FDE, on the other hand, do not. Each of these logics is reflexive, and so in particular they all validate p \;/\; p. Any logic that validates p \;/\; p and obeys conditional proof must also validate \;/\; p \rightarrow p, since the following is an instance of conditional proof:

But take a strong Kleene valuation w where w(p) = \mathsf {\mathbf {*}}. This gives us w(p \rightarrow p) = \mathsf {\mathbf {*}}, so w is a K3 counterexample (but note well, not an LP counterexample!) to the argument \;/\; p \rightarrow p. Thus, that argument is not valid in K3, and since FDE is weaker than K3, it is not valid in those either. Since all these logics do validate p \;/\; p, though, this means that they don't obey conditional proof.

Notice too that the empty logic, while it is weaker than K3, does obey conditional proof. (Remember logicians' weird habit number 2!) Because the empty logic does not validate p \;/\; p, the fact that it also does not validate \;/\; p \rightarrow p is no obstacle to its obeying conditional proof.

How does conditional proof go in our new logics? Suppose that we have some proof of an argument \Gamma , A \;/\; B. That is, we have some proof with the conclusion B, all of whose open assumptions are contained in \Gamma , A. Then we can extend this proof with a single step of \rightarrow I, discharging any open assumptions of A there happened to be, reaching the conclusion A \rightarrow B. Since we've discharged any open assumptions of A, the resulting proof will have all its open assumptions contained in \Gamma ; thus, it is a proof of \Gamma \;/\; A \rightarrow B. For example, consider the following proof of p, q \lor r \;/\; (p \land q) \lor r:
We can extend this proof with one step of \rightarrow I\ to reach the following proof of p \;/\; (q \lor r) \rightarrow ((p \land q) \lor r):
And the same kind of thing will work no matter what sentences or proofs we're dealing with. So minimal logic, intuitionistic logic, and classical logic all obey conditional proof as well.

That all might make intuitionistic and minimal logics seem more like classical logic than our various other logics: after all, of the logics that we've seen, only these three both validate all instances of modus ponens and obey conditional proof. But we can turn the point around: those other logics all share certain similarities with classical logic that intuitionistic and minimal logic don't share.
Intuitionistic logic and minimal logic, however, validate only the last three of these, not the first.

As always, by this I mean that there are some instances of the first that are invalid in these logics. (Some other instances remain valid.) For an example, 
eg (p \land q) \;/\; 
eg p \lor 
eg q is invalid in intuitionistic logic, but valid in every matrix logic we've looked at in these readings so far.

(It is possible, however to make matrix logics where this fails. Indeed, intuitionistic logic itself is a matrix logic! We won't look at a matrix for intuitionistic logic, however, since all such matrices require an infinite structure, which can be a pain. We'll look at a different way to build models for intuitionistic logic in the next lecture.)
It can help to look at a proof of this argument in NK to see what's up:

Note the uses of the rule LEM here. That means that this is only a proof in NK, not also in NJ or NM. (As there is also a use of \bot E, that's another reason this isn't a proof in NM.) On its own, that gives no guarantee that there isn't some other proof in NJ of this argument; but in fact there isn't. And I think, by meditating on this piece of the above proof, you can start to see why that's so:
If we wanted to discharge both the p and the q, we could even do that, arriving here for example:

This is still not a proof of 
eg (p \land q) \;/\; 
eg p \lor 
eg q; it is instead a proof of 
eg (p \land q) \;/\; p ightarrow 
eg q. In intuitionistic and minimal logic, assuming 
eg (p \land q) is enough to get us to the conclusion that p implies 
eg q, but it's still not enough to get us to the conclusion 
eg p \lor 
eg q. That means that there's an important difference in these logics between 
eg A \lor B and A ightarrow B: these are very much not interchangeable in intuitionistic and minimal logic.

So there are commonalities intuitionistic logic has with classical logic that are not shared with the other matrix logics we've looked at, and there are commonalities those other logics have with classical logic that are not shared by intuitionistic logic. Intuitionistic and minimal logics thus give us a new kind of family of logics, different from the ones we've seen before. (Logics like these are often called "constructive" logics.)

This week focused on taking some stock of our proof systems, and the resulting logics. We've seen how to get a logic from a proof system, learned a little bit about some of the properties of intuitionistic and minimal logic, and looked at two key philosophical ideas: one about realism and antirealism, and one about ideas of meaning.

html:ul.html:li:
Section A: Preliminaries
Lecture 1: Introduction and languages
Lecture 2: Functions and substitutions
Lecture 3: Sets, arguments, and logics

Section B: Structures and matrices
Lecture 4: Structures
Lecture 5: Matrices and counterexamples
Lecture 6: Using matrices to compare logics
Lecture 7: The limits of matrices

Section C: Exploring intuitionistic logic
Lecture 8: Natural deduction
Lecture 9: Intuitionistic logic
Lecture 10: Intuitionistic models
Lecture 11: Harmony
